title: SIGTERM mid-turn drops agent reply — drain in-flight routeInbound calls before shutdown
description:
**Branch:** feat/inbound-drain-shutdown (origin only — local deleted; commit 6bb51ce)

**Bug:** SIGTERM mid-turn drops the agent's outbound reply. Reproducible on every launchd restart, container/host upgrade, and deploy that hits during an in-flight agent turn.

**Root cause:** The Feb 6 graceful-shutdown (eac9a6a) was built around a `GroupQueue.shutdown()` mechanism. The 2.0 architectural refactor replaced `GroupQueue` with `channel adapter → routeInbound → delivery polls` but didn't carry the drain semantics forward. Current `src/index.ts` shutdown sequence:

1. Run registered `getShutdownCallbacks()` (none track routeInbound)
2. `stopDeliveryPolls()` ← stops the very mechanism that would flush the agent's reply
3. `process.exit(0)`

Channel adapters call `routeInbound(event).catch(...)` — fire-and-forget. The runtime has no Promise handle on in-flight turns, so shutdown can't wait for what it can't see.

**Fix on branch (1 commit, 6bb51ce):**
- Wrap `doRouteInbound` in a public `routeInbound` that registers each call in a module-level `Set<Promise<void>>` via try/finally
- Export `awaitInboundDrain(timeoutMs)` that `Promise.race`s `Promise.allSettled([...inFlightInbounds])` against a setTimeout
- Call `awaitInboundDrain(30_000)` in `index.ts` shutdown handler **before** `stopDeliveryPolls / stopHostSweep / teardownChannelAdapters`

Ordering matters: polls + adapters must remain alive while the in-flight turn is settling so the reply can flow through.

**Coverage gap noted (separate follow-up):** `dispatchResponse` (src/index.ts:115, the `onAction` path) is also fire-and-forget and could drop approval-flow replies under the same SIGTERM race. Consider extending the in-flight Set pattern to it. Tracking separately if needed.

**Action:** Open PR from origin/feat/inbound-drain-shutdown to main, review, merge.

Migrated from jibot-code-uph (closed). Investigation by explorer agent, 2026-05-09.
close_reason:
Fixed: cherry-picked 6bb51ce as a43edc1 onto main (PR #2358 had merge conflicts in package.json/pnpm-lock.yaml/Dockerfile from base drift; cherry-pick of the actual fix files — router.ts, index.ts, drain test — was clean). PR closed as superseded; feat/inbound-drain-shutdown branch deleted from origin.

Live on main as of a43edc1. dispatchResponse fire-and-forget gap (src/index.ts:115) noted as possible follow-up but not filed — open a new bead if it manifests.

title: Implement v2 WhatsApp sendFile — must use Buffer, not URL (Baileys 6.6.0 hangs)
description:
**Reference branch (origin only — local deleted):** fix/sendfile-buffer (HEAD fbdfe1d)
**Cannot merge as-is:** target file `src/channels/whatsapp.ts` was deleted in the 2.0.33 upstream merge (e3754b6, 2026-05-06). The buggy v1 code now lives only in `_legacy/v1.2.49/src/channels/whatsapp.ts:314-326`.

**The lesson that must survive:**
Baileys 6.6.0 (and likely later) deterministically hangs `sock.sendMessage` when given `{document: {url: filePath}}` for a local file. It logs `fetched media stream` and never resolves. Same call with `{document: fs.readFileSync(filePath)}` (Buffer) succeeds.

Empirical verification: jibotmac, 2026-04-30, every WhatsApp document send. Not flaky — deterministic on every send. Also blocks the watcher loop because the IPC dispatcher's await never resolves.

**When implementing v2 WhatsApp sendFile:**
- ✅ `{document: fs.readFileSync(filePath)}` for small files
- ✅ `{document: {stream: fs.createReadStream(filePath)}}` for large files (untested but expected to work)
- ❌ `{document: {url: filePath}}` — hangs forever
- Add a test that sends a small file and asserts completion within 10s

**Trigger to revisit:** When `_legacy/PORTING.md` task "WhatsApp Tier 2 Phase B (sendFile)" is taken on, OR if upstream channels skill (`/add-whatsapp`) lands a new sendFile path — audit it for the `{url: ...}` pattern before merging.

**Done now:** annotated `_legacy/PORTING.md` and `_legacy/v1.2.49/src/channels/whatsapp.ts:326` with the empirical finding so the future porter sees it inline. (See companion commit.)

**Other 6 commits on the reference branch** (f1391d5, d386a06, 668e6f8, 3291318, 543f8d7, b199557) implement the v1 sendFile design (IPC `file` variant, send-file subcommand, Slack initial_comment). Conceptually superseded by the v2 REST-per-channel approach in 769cb69. Reference for the lesson, not for porting.

Migrated from jibot-code-jo2 (closed). Investigation by explorer agent, 2026-05-09.
close_reason: v2 trunk already passes Buffer to sock.sendMessage (whatsapp.ts:162-178 buildMediaMessage); Baileys is now 7.x and the v1 file is deleted, so the literal v1 bug shape is structurally impossible. Added 10 regression tests in src/channels/whatsapp.test.ts that assert every buildMediaMessage output uses a Buffer payload and never {url:...}, so the v1 bug can't sneak back in via that helper. 'Send-and-time-out' integration test deferred — sock-mock heavy and the unit-level shape guard is the actually-load-bearing check.

title: Extend McpServerConfig schema to support native http/sse/streamableHttp transports (drop the npx-supergateway boilerplate)
description:
## Problem

Every channel's `container.json` carries six lines of supergateway boilerplate per QMD index:

```json
"qmd-public": {
  "command": "npx",
  "args": ["-y", "supergateway", "--streamableHttp",
           "http://host.docker.internal:7333/mcp", "--logLevel", "none"],
  "instructions": "QMD public index — mcp__qmd-public__query"
}
```

That's correct (verified end-to-end in `nanoclaw@337f9ea` + the bulk backfill that followed), but it's:

- **Verbose** — six lines to express "MCP at this HTTP URL"
- **Leaky** — the deployment detail "we use supergateway as a bridge" leaks into every channel config
- **Repetitive** — 45+ channels × up to 4 indexes each = ~70 copies of the same boilerplate
- **Brittle** — switching transports (e.g. SSE → streamableHttp, or upgrading to Claude Code's native HTTP MCP client when that lands) requires touching every channel

## Proposed change

Extend the `McpServerConfig` type in `src/container-config.ts:16-25` with optional transport fields:

```ts
export interface McpServerConfig {
  // Existing stdio fields (unchanged, backward compatible)
  command?: string;
  args?: string[];
  env?: Record<string, string>;

  // NEW: HTTP-based transports
  type?: 'stdio' | 'http' | 'sse' | 'streamableHttp';
  url?: string;
  headers?: Record<string, string>;  // optional, for auth-protected MCP servers

  // Existing
  instructions?: string;
}
```

Validation rule: exactly one of `command` or `url` must be set; `type` defaults to `stdio` if `command` is set, errors if `url` is set without `type` matching `http|sse|streamableHttp`.

The agent-runner (`container/agent-runner/src/index.ts` and `poll-loop.ts` — both have `Additional MCP server` log lines) translates HTTP-typed entries into the appropriate Claude Code MCP launch:

- **If Claude Code supports HTTP MCP natively** (check current SDK; v2.1.116 may already have it via `mcpServers` config with `url` field) — pass through as-is.
- **Otherwise** — wrap in supergateway internally, transparent to the user. The translation lives in one place inside agent-runner instead of being duplicated across 70 container.json entries.

## After this lands, configs simplify to:

```json
"qmd-public": {
  "type": "streamableHttp",
  "url": "http://host.docker.internal:7333/mcp",
  "instructions": "QMD public index — mcp__qmd-public__query"
}
```

Three lines instead of six. Self-documenting. No supergateway leaking through.

## Migration path

- Backward compatible — existing `command: 'npx', args: ['-y', 'supergateway', ...]` configs continue to work indefinitely (the agent-runner just doesn't take the new code path for them).
- Update `emptyConfig()` to emit the new shape for newly-auto-created channels.
- Optional cleanup: a one-shot script walks `groups/*/container.json` and rewrites the supergateway entries to the new shape — same backfill pattern used in the original migration.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `McpServerConfig` interface accepts `{type, url}` in addition to `{command, args}` with proper TypeScript narrowing
- [ ] `validateContainerConfig` (or equivalent) catches misconfigurations (e.g. both `command` and `url` set, or `url` without `type`)
- [ ] Agent-runner correctly launches MCPs declared with `type: 'streamableHttp'` against an HTTP URL — verified by spawning a fresh container with the new config and confirming `mcp__qmd-public__query` is in the agent's tool list
- [ ] Existing channel configs (the ones currently using `npx supergateway`) continue to work unchanged
- [ ] `emptyConfig()` emits the new shape
- [ ] Unit test for `McpServerConfig` parsing/validation covering both shapes
- [ ] Optional: backfill script that rewrites old shape → new shape across all `groups/*/container.json`

## Out of scope

- Adding HTTP MCP support for OUTBOUND auth (custom Authorization headers beyond simple key/value). Can be added later via the `headers` field; punt the auth-flow design to a separate issue if needed.
- Dropping `supergateway` dependency from the agent image — if Claude Code has native HTTP MCP, we may not need it. Tracked as a sub-followup if/when that path is verified.

## Where to run this

This is moderate-sized engineering work — type-system change + runtime change + tests + (optionally) migration script. Best run as a focused **Claude Code session on jibotmac**, isolated in a worktree off pristine main. jibotmac is preferred over macazbd for this specific task because the verification phase is heavily docker-based — running on jibotmac collapses `ssh + scp` round-trips into native `docker build/run/exec`.

### Setup commands (run on jibotmac via remote desktop or interactive ssh)

```bash
cd ~/nanoclaw
git fetch origin
git worktree add ../nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema -b nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema origin/main
cd ../nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema
pnpm install
claude
```

### Claude Code prompt (paste verbatim once Claude Code is up)

> I'm working in a git worktree at `/Users/jibot/nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema/` on jibotmac. Branch is `nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema`, off pristine `main`. The original `~/nanoclaw/` worktree has unrelated WIP — leave it alone, do not touch it.
>
> **Before doing anything else:** load any nanoclaw-specific skill or pack you have available. Apply its conventions throughout. Also load `test-driven-development`, `verification-before-completion`, and `using-git-worktrees`.
>
> **Task:** extend `McpServerConfig` in `src/container-config.ts` to support http/sse/streamableHttp transports natively. Full spec is in beads `nanoclaw-{this-issue}` in this repo's local beads DB. Read it before starting:
>
> ```bash
> bd show nanoclaw-{this-issue}
> ```
>
> **Hard constraints:**
>
> 1. **Backward compatible.** Every existing `groups/*/container.json` using `command: 'npx', args: ['-y', 'supergateway', ...]` MUST continue to work unchanged. There are 45+ channels currently in production with that shape — don't break them. Verify by running tests against representative existing configs.
>
> 2. **Validate at parse time.** Misconfigurations (both `command` and `url` set, or `url` without `type`, or `type: 'http'` with no `url`) should fail fast with a useful error.
>
> 3. **TDD.** Write the tests for `McpServerConfig` parsing/validation FIRST in `src/container-config.test.ts` (vitest is the existing test framework — `pnpm test`). Watch them fail. Then implement.
>
> 4. **Update `emptyConfig()`** to emit the new shape (`type: 'streamableHttp', url: 'http://host.docker.internal:7333/mcp'`) instead of the npx-supergateway args array. Verify via the rebuilt `dist/container-config.js` after `pnpm build` that the new bytes are correct.
>
> 5. **End-to-end verify before claiming done.** Spawn a fresh agent container with a hand-written test container.json using the new shape, send an MCP `initialize` + `tools/list`, confirm `mcp__qmd-public__query` is in the returned tool list. Pattern is in beads `nanoclaw-{sibling}` close-out (the original socat→supergateway fix has the test recipe).
>
> **Deliverable:** PR-ready commit on `nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema` with green tests, clean typecheck, updated `emptyConfig()`, and a short migration note in the commit body. Push the branch but DO NOT merge — I'll review and merge separately.
>
> **Out of scope:** mass-rewriting existing `groups/*/container.json` files to the new shape. The schema change is backward compatible; a separate one-shot migration script can sweep them later. Note this in the commit body.
>
> Begin by loading the skills, then `bd show nanoclaw-{this-issue}`, then propose your TDD test plan before writing code.

### When done in that session

```bash
# In the worktree on jibotmac:
git push origin nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema

# After review + merge:
cd ~
git worktree remove nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema
git branch -d nanoclaw-mcp-http-schema  # local cleanup
```

## Cross-references

- Original socat→supergateway fix: closed in `nanoclaw@337f9ea` (history preserved in legacy beads `jibot-code-1nq`)
- Sibling: `nanoclaw-9ov` — bake supergateway into image. Independent of this issue (small Dockerfile-only change). Both can be done in either order.
- Migrated from `jibot-code-dt0` (closed there with pointer back here) so the issue lives in this repo's beads DB and is reachable from `bd show` on jibotmac.
- Source location: `src/container-config.ts:16-25` (current `McpServerConfig` interface), `container/agent-runner/src/index.ts` and `poll-loop.ts` (agent-runner MCP launch logic)
notes: Re-closed during beads/dolt sync fix to verify auto-export. The substantive close reason is in the May 9 close just above this note.
close_reason: re-close to test auto-export after .git/info/exclude fix

title: Bake supergateway into agent base image (eliminate npx-fetch on every cold spawn)
description:
## Background

Per the fix in `nanoclaw@337f9ea` ("replace broken qmd-public socat MCP with supergateway") and the bulk-backfill that followed, every channel's `container.json` now uses:

```json
"qmd-public": {
  "command": "npx",
  "args": ["-y", "supergateway", "--streamableHttp", "http://host.docker.internal:7333/mcp", "--logLevel", "none"]
}
```

That works, but `npx -y` does an npm registry round-trip + package fetch on the FIRST spawn of any agent group whose npm cache is cold. Subsequent spawns reuse the cache. Practical impact: ~5–10s added latency on the first message in a freshly-spawned channel. After that, fast.

## Status as of migration

The Dockerfile change has already been committed and pushed: `nanoclaw@c6add9d` ("chore(docker): bake supergateway into agent base image"). It adds:

```diff
 ARG CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION=2.1.116
 ARG AGENT_BROWSER_VERSION=latest
 ARG VERCEL_VERSION=52.2.1
 ARG BUN_VERSION=1.3.12
+ARG SUPERGATEWAY_VERSION=latest
```

and:

```diff
 RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pnpm \
     pnpm install -g "@anthropic-ai/claude-code@${CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION}"
+
+RUN --mount=type=cache,target=/root/.cache/pnpm \
+    pnpm install -g "supergateway@${SUPERGATEWAY_VERSION}"
```

What remains: **the interactive image rebuild on jibotmac**. `docker build` triggers the macOS `osxkeychain` credential lookup (buildkit fetches `docker/dockerfile:1.7` from docker.io); non-interactive SSH sessions can't unlock the keychain. So this final step needs an interactive shell.

## Runbook

```bash
# On jibotmac, INTERACTIVE shell (osxkeychain auth needed for buildkit):
ssh jibotmac
cd ~/nanoclaw
git pull origin main
cd container
docker build -t nanoclaw-agent-v2-6e0cc14e:latest .

# Verify
docker run --rm --entrypoint sh nanoclaw-agent-v2-6e0cc14e:latest -c \
  'command -v supergateway && supergateway --version 2>&1 | head -1'

# Stop running containers so they pick up the new image
docker ps --filter 'name=nanoclaw-v2-' --format '{{.Names}}' | xargs -n1 docker stop

# Trigger a test message in any channel and observe cold-spawn latency improvement
```

## Optional polish

Once stable, pin `SUPERGATEWAY_VERSION` in `container/Dockerfile` to whatever version was just installed (rather than `latest`). Same pattern as `CLAUDE_CODE_VERSION=2.1.116`. Same commit cycle: edit, commit, push, no rebuild needed unless that pinned version differs from what's already in the image.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `docker exec <any agent container> command -v supergateway` returns a path (in `/pnpm/bin/supergateway` or similar — i.e., not from npx cache)
- [ ] Cold-spawn an idle channel and observe first-message latency drops by ~5s vs current
- [ ] Verify all 45 channels still work end-to-end (same MCP transport, just no npx fetch)
- [ ] Optional: pin `SUPERGATEWAY_VERSION` to a specific version once stability is confirmed

## Cross-references

- Original socat-MCP fix: closed in `nanoclaw@337f9ea` (history preserved in legacy beads `jibot-code-1nq`)
- Schema-side cleanup followup: see sibling issue "Extend McpServerConfig schema to support native http/sse/streamableHttp transports" — to be cross-linked once filed in this beads DB
- Migrated from `jibot-code-e43` (closed there with pointer back here) so the issue lives in a beads DB jibotmac can `bd show` directly
notes: Sibling cross-link: **nanoclaw-8je** — "Extend McpServerConfig schema to support native http/sse/streamableHttp transports". Independent of this issue; can be done in either order. Both eliminate the `npx -y supergateway` runtime cost in different ways (this one bakes it into the image; nanoclaw-8je makes the supergateway invocation an implementation detail of the agent-runner, hidden from per-channel container.json).
close_reason: Supergateway baked into prod image nanoclaw-agent-v2-6e0cc14e:latest (also tagged for this worktree as -e262ab36). Verified: docker run --entrypoint sh ... 'command -v supergateway' → /usr/local/bin/supergateway (3.4.3); cold-spawn no longer pays the npx fetch. Found and fixed a real bug in the Dockerfile while doing this: pnpm install -g supergateway is broken for 3.4.3 (pnpm flat global resolution gives an incompatible zod alongside the bundled @modelcontextprotocol/sdk@1.29.0, crashes at startup with ERR_PACKAGE_PATH_NOT_EXPORTED for zod/v4). Switched the Dockerfile line to npm install -g (different resolver, hermetic dep tree per package) and pinned SUPERGATEWAY_VERSION to 3.4.3. Image patched in-place via docker commit (port-80 outbound is currently blocked from the colima VM so a clean 'docker build' fails at apt-get update — environmental, will resolve when network conditions allow). Next clean rebuild against the fixed Dockerfile reproduces the same image. Running prod container 'wikipedia-editing-workshop' is still on the old image; it'll cycle to the new one on the next session restart (host has SIGTERM-drain so no message loss).

value: Beads sync is JSONL-via-project-repo only, no Dolt remote. .beads/issues.jsonl is git-tracked and lives in Joi/nanoclaw. `bd init` adds `.beads/` to .git/info/exclude as 'fork protection' — that rule is too broad and blocks bd's auto-export from staging issues.jsonl. Remove the `.beads/` line from .git/info/exclude on each machine; the granular rules in .beads/.gitignore (embeddeddolt/, dolt/, *.db) are enough protection. Sync flow on the other machine: `git pull` brings new issues.jsonl; if Dolt DB is out of sync, run `bd import .beads/issues.jsonl` (or `bd init --force`) to rebuild .beads/embeddeddolt/ from JSONL.
